cataloging_subject.label: English Metrical Homilies
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection encoded from John Small's 1862 English Metrical Homilies: its title-page content and complete printed verse text on pp. 1-171, comprising the Prologue, Ratio, and the first thirteen Northern Homily Cycle homilies from Advent through the Purification. The selection excludes Small's manuscript facsimile plate, introduction and manuscript description, blank p. 172, notes on pp. 173-188, and remaining scan matter. It is neither the complete Northern Homily Cycle, the complete 1862 publication, one physical manuscript, the U-M encoding, nor the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: English Metrical Homilies
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
catalog_summary.date: Underlying cycle probably composed c. 1295-1306; edited selection published 1862
catalog_summary.region: Northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; witness copying and Edinburgh publication are recorded separately

assertion.notes: This is the edition-level selection title, not a medieval title attested in the three witnesses and not evidence that John Small authored the homilies.

titles.value: English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century, with an Introduction and Notes
Northern Homily Cycle: Prologue through the Purification
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus cataloging, from modern work identification and the encoded boundary

assertion.notes: A discovery label clarifying the selection's relation to the larger cycle; it is not represented as an original title.

agents.name: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
notes: The author's identity is unknown. A proposed Augustinian canon context is a modern hypothesis, not a known personal identity or secure religious-order attribution.

agents.name: John Small
notes: 1828-1886; editor, selector, transcriber, and collator of the 1862 edition.

scope.description: The medieval Prologue, Ratio, Gospel paraphrases, homilies, and exempla in the selected opening of the cycle

assertion.notes: Small's discussion of Richard Rolle and other possible origins is historically important but superseded and inconclusive; it does not justify a named-author record.

scope.description: Selection and arrangement of the Edinburgh fragment with introduction, plate, text, and notes
The printed medieval text, with witness-specific dependencies recorded in the linked lineage manifest
Principal Edinburgh copy text, substantial supplies from Gg.5.31 and Ashmole 42, and comparison readings in the notes
Underlying Northern Homily Cycle material represented by the selected opening

assertion.notes: A reasoned modern window rather than a dated authorial document; the cycle's date and author remain unknown.

scope.description: Earliest surviving witness of the selected opening in the RCPE manuscript

assertion.notes: A witness-production date, not an automatic composition date.

scope.description: Small's edited selection as cataloged here

places.label: Northern England
notes: Broad work-level origin used by current Northern Homily Cycle scholarship; distinct from later manuscript repositories.

places.label: Yorkshire
notes: A possible refinement of the cycle's origin, kept tentative. The North Riding localization of the RCPE copying hand is witness evidence rather than direct proof of composition here.

assertion.notes: A broad work-level localization, not a statement about where the Edinburgh codex was copied or later held.
Modern scholarship suggests Yorkshire as a possibility, while LAEME more securely localizes the RCPE copying hand to the North Riding; those claims are not conflated.

scope.description: Prologue, Ratio, Gospel paraphrases, homilies, and exempla
Embedded Gospel rubrics, quotations, and an encoded thirty-three-line Latin verse passage within the Second Sunday of Advent homily
English-language edition title page and modern editorial framing retained by the encoding, not the primary language of the medieval text

form_statements.description: The medieval selection is verse, predominantly rhyming octosyllabic couplets; editorial title-page prose is paratext rather than a prose content item.

genre_statements.label: Gospel paraphrase
genre_statements.scheme: METS edition categories and General Corpus local genre

scope.description: Narrative examples embedded across the homily selection
Saints' legends and miracles used as exemplary narratives across the selection

assertion.notes: The lay and oral-use context is plausible from form and content but should not be converted into a documented performance event.

scope.description: A recurrent subject in selected passages across the homily sequence
A recurrent subject in selected passages across the homily sequence
Saints, miracles, and exemplary narratives embedded across the homily sequence

summaries.value: An edited opening of the anonymous Northern Homily Cycle, a Middle English verse-sermon sequence arranged for the liturgical year. After a Prologue and Ratio, each selected item begins from a Latin Gospel rubric and develops a Middle English Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and often one or more exempla. The selection proceeds from the first Sunday of Advent through the Purification, addressing repentance, confession, mercy, wealth and poverty, clerical conduct, saints, and miracles.

Prologue; Ratio; homilies for the first through fourth Sundays of Advent; the Nativity; the Sunday within the octave of the Nativity; the Epiphany; the Sunday within its octave; the first through fourth Sundays after its octave; and the Purification of the Virgin Mary.

The cataloging unit is exactly Small's title-page-plus-pp. 1-171 selection as encoded by U-M. The complete 1862 book, Northern Homily Cycle, three manuscripts, digital encoding, repository artifact, scan, and manuscript plate remain separate descriptive and lineage layers.

The U-M page's bibliographic Author label is not propagated as medieval authorship: the title page names John Small as editor, and current scholarship leaves the cycle anonymous. Small's primary Edinburgh witness and supplementary Cambridge and Ashmole witnesses are attributed only at the exact source scopes supported by his notes.

extent.description: The encoded medieval content has fifteen cataloged items: a 128-line Prologue, a 40-line Ratio, and thirteen homilies totaling 3,948 further lines. The seventeen XML LG elements include a separately nested thirty-three-line Latin passage in the Second Sunday of Advent homily. The homily count remains thirteen; the manifest item total also counts Prologue and Ratio. Title-page paratext is excluded from these item and line totals.

content_parts.label: Prologue
content_parts.summary: Introduces the cycle's pastoral purpose, audience, and use of Gospel teaching in English verse.

content_parts.label: Ratio
content_parts.summary: A compact statement of the work's rationale and method before the first liturgical homily.

content_parts.label: The First Sunday of Advent
content_parts.summary: Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral teaching, and exemplary narrative for the first Sunday of Advent.

content_parts.label: The Second Sunday of Advent
content_parts.summary: Gospel paraphrase and homiletic exposition for the second Sunday of Advent, including a separately encoded Latin verse passage.

content_parts.label: The Third Sunday of Advent
content_parts.summary: Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and exemplary material for the third Sunday of Advent.

content_parts.label: The Fourth Sunday of Advent
content_parts.summary: Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and exemplary material for the fourth Sunday of Advent.

content_parts.label: The Nativity of Our Lord
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for Christmas, with doctrinal exposition and exemplary narrative.

content_parts.label: The Sunday within the Octave of the Nativity
content_parts.summary: An extended verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the Sunday within the Christmas octave.
notes: Small's explicitly sourced Cambridge and Ashmole supplies fall within this printed-page range; exact spans are recorded in lineage rather than homogenized here.

content_parts.label: The Epiphany
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase, exposition, pastoral application, and exemplary material for the Epiphany.

content_parts.label: The Sunday within the Octave of the Epiphany
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homiletic teaching for the Sunday within the Epiphany octave.

content_parts.label: The First Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the first Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

content_parts.label: The Second Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the second Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

content_parts.label: The Third Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the third Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

content_parts.label: The Fourth Sunday after the Octave of the Epiphany
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase and homily for the fourth Sunday after the Epiphany octave.

content_parts.label: The Purification of the Virgin Mary
content_parts.summary: Verse Gospel paraphrase, exposition, and exempla for the Purification; the selected fragment ends during the cycle's Purification material.
notes: The printed and encoded endpoint follows the surviving fragment; the complete Northern Homily Cycle continues beyond this selection. Small's final passage from p. 167 line 22 is supplied from Gg.5.31.

related_works.title: The Northern Homily Cycle
notes: CME00029 is Small's edited opening fragment, not the complete cycle of homilies for the liturgical year.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00029.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for identifiers, title-page content, Prologue, Ratio, thirteen homily divisions, headings, Middle English and Latin content, printed-page markers, literal bracketed supplies, and review date.

evidence.citation: Structural XPath, text, page-marker, and hash inspection of CME00029.xml.
evidence.locator: counts of DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, PB, NOTE, HI, ABBR, SUP, GAP, UNCLEAR, square brackets, and lines by body division
evidence.summary: Reproducibly establishes 4,116 lines in seventeen verse groups, the fifteen-part content summary, per-part line counts and print-page ranges, one thirty-three-line nested Latin passage, 172 page markers, and literal rather than semantic encoding of twenty-seven balanced square-bracket spans.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00029, English Metrical Homilies.
evidence.summary: Official item evidence for CME/UMDL identity, title, 1862 Edinburgh imprint, subjects, public-domain status, and digital text. Its generic bibliographic Author label for John Small is corrected to editor from direct title-page and scholarly evidence.

evidence.citation: John Small, ed., English Metrical Homilies (Edinburgh: William Paterson, 1862), complete Harvard/Google/Internet Archive scan, item englishmetrical00smalgoog.
evidence.locator: title page; plate; pp. v-xxii; printed pp. 1-188; CME selection at title page and pp. 1-171
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for title, imprint, John Small's editorial responsibility, complete volume boundary, content headings, line and page locations, the discontiguous U-M selection, and the distinction between full print scan and partial manuscript plate.

evidence.citation: John Small, Introduction, Description of Manuscript, and Notes, in English Metrical Homilies (1862), pp. v-xxii and 173-188.
evidence.summary: Small's direct statements establish the edition boundary, principal Edinburgh source, supplementary Cambridge and Ashmole supplies, comparison practice, manuscript description, anonymous and uncertain medieval authorship, and his editor, compiler, transcriber, and collator roles.

evidence.citation: Anne B. Thompson, ed., The Northern Homily Cycle (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2008), introduction and manuscript descriptions.
evidence.summary: Current specialist evidence for the Northern Homily Cycle's identity, anonymous authorship, tentative c. 1295-1306 date, northern or perhaps Yorkshire origin, liturgical structure, verse form, genres, subjects, intended pastoral context, manuscript tradition, and the fragmentary scope of Small's edition.

evidence.citation: Linguistic Atlas of Early Middle English, Chapter 3, profile discussion for the Edinburgh Royal College of Physicians Cursor Mundi manuscript.
evidence.summary: Specialist linguistic evidence localizing the RCPE Northern Homily Cycle copying hand to the North Riding. The catalog record uses it only to qualify, not prove, a Yorkshire composition hypothesis.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00029.
evidence.summary: Linked evidence separating repository XML, U-M encoding, Small edition, three manuscript witnesses, complete print scan, partial manuscript plate, and later scholarship, with exact source-supply spans, access negatives, local copies, and rights layers.

open_questions.question: Can complete current images of the RCPE, Gg.5.31, and Ashmole 42 manuscripts establish exact folio or page crosswalks for every part of Small's copy text and supplied passages?
next_steps: Use the complete-object request routes and shot-list boundaries recorded in lineage.
Preserve every provider label and the Gg.5.31 competing pagination systems.
Map Small's print ranges and brackets only after direct image comparison.

open_questions.question: What current RCPE shelfmark and catalog description should supplement the scholarly designation MS of Cursor Mundi?
next_steps: Ask RCPE Library and Archive to identify the object from Small's edition, Drummond's 1741 bequest, and the Northern Homily Cycle locus ff. 16r-36v.
Add only a repository-confirmed shelfmark and preserve historical designations separately.

open_questions.question: How should competing modern arguments about the cycle's exact composition date, Yorkshire origin, Augustinian context, author, and intended performance setting be represented after fuller review?
next_steps: Review the underlying Carver, Heffernan, and linguistic arguments rather than relying only on Thompson's synthesis.
Record materially competing claims with attribution and confidence.
Keep anonymous authorship, c. 1295-1306, Yorkshire, Augustinian, and lay oral-delivery statements qualified until that review.

open_questions.question: When and by whom was CME00029 first keyed, and how does the checked-in XML differ from U-M's current source XML?
next_steps: Retrieve and compare the exact current U-M XML bitstream.
Document keying, proofreading, structural revision, and publication dates from U-M records.
Keep the 2016 date scoped to repository review.

notes: The preferred title describes Small's 1862 edited selection rather than a medieval witness title or the complete Northern Homily Cycle.
John Small remains edition-level editor, selector, transcriber, and collator; the anonymous medieval poet remains work-level author.
The thirteen homilies are stable content parts, with Prologue and Ratio separately modeled and internal Gospel-paraphrase, exposition, and exemplum boundaries summarized rather than over-segmented.
The RCPE codex, its Northern Homily Cycle hand and folios, Small's three-snippet plate, and the complete print scan are distinct physical, textual, editorial, and digital layers.

The record remains partial because all three complete manuscript objects, exact supplementary-witness loci, the RCPE current shelfmark, fuller competing authorship and dating scholarship, and exact U-M production history remain unresolved.
